questions.question: Explain symmetric encryption and list its five components.
keyPoints: Symmetric encryption uses the same secret key for encryption and decryption
Components: 1) Plaintext (original message), 2) Encryption algorithm, 3) Secret key, 4) Ciphertext (encrypted message), 5) Decryption algorithm
Also known as secret-key or conventional encryption

questions.question: Differentiate between block cipher and stream cipher with examples.
keyPoints: Block cipher: encrypts data in fixed-size blocks (e.g., 64/128 bits) - AES, DES, Blowfish
Stream cipher: encrypts data bit-by-bit or byte-by-byte - RC4
Block ciphers use modes of operation (ECB, CBC) to process multiple blocks
Stream ciphers suitable for real-time data encryption

questions.question: What is the Key Distribution Problem in symmetric encryption? How can it be solved?
keyPoints: The challenge of securely sharing the secret key between communicating parties
If key is intercepted, entire communication is compromised
Solutions: 1) Manual key distribution, 2) Using asymmetric encryption to share symmetric keys, 3) Using Key Exchange Protocols (Diffie-Hellman), 4) Using Key Distribution Centers (KDC)

questions.question: Explain ECB mode of operation and state why it is considered insecure.
keyPoints: ECB (Electronic Codebook) is the simplest mode where each plaintext block is encrypted independently
All identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks
This reveals patterns in the data - unsuitable for encrypted images or structured data
Does not provide diffusion - statistical properties of plaintext are preserved in ciphertext
Should not be used for sensitive data encryption

questions.question: What is AES? Explain its key features.
keyPoints: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) selected by NIST in 2001 as successor to DES
Symmetric block cipher with 128-bit block size
Supports three key sizes: 128, 192, and 256 bits with 10, 12, 14 rounds respectively
Uses Substitution-Permutation Network (SPN) structure
Currently the most widely used encryption standard worldwide
Approved by U.S. government for protecting classified information

questions.question: Define padding in block ciphers. Explain PKCS#7 padding.
keyPoints: Block ciphers require input data to be exact multiple of block size
Padding adds extra bytes to the last block to meet block size requirement
PKCS#7 adds bytes equal to number of padding bytes needed (1-16 for AES)
If data is already multiple of block size, a full block of padding is added
Example: If 3 bytes needed to reach 16-byte block, add 03 03 03

questions.question: Compare DES and AES on the basis of key size, block size, and security.
keyPoints: DES: 56-bit key (effective), 64-bit block size, now considered insecure (broken by brute force)
AES: 128/192/256-bit key options, 128-bit block size, considered secure
DES uses Feistel structure; AES uses Substitution-Permutation Network
AES is faster than DES in software implementations
AES designed to resist known attacks; DES has known vulnerabilities

questions.question: Explain CBC (Cipher Block Chaining) mode in detail. Include initialization vector, encryption/decryption processes, and advantages over ECB.
keyPoints: CBC uses Initialization Vector (IV) for randomization
Encryption: Ci = Ek(Pi ⊕ C(i-1)), where C0 = IV
Decryption: Pi = Dk(Ci) ⊕ C(i-1)
Each ciphertext block depends on all previous plaintext blocks
Advantages over ECB: 1) Conceals patterns, 2) Provides diffusion, 3) More secure
Disadvantages: 1) Sequential processing (cannot parallelize), 2) Error propagation (one block error affects next two blocks)
IV should be random and unpredictable but not necessarily secret

questions.question: What is One-Time Pad? Explain why it is considered perfectly secure and what are its practical limitations.
keyPoints: One-Time Pad (OTP) uses a random key as long as the message
Key must be: 1) Random, 2) Same length as message, 3) Used only once, 4) Kept completely secret
Encryption: C = M ⊕ K; Decryption: M = C ⊕ K
Perfect secrecy: Intercepting C reveals no information about M (unconditional security)
Proven by Claude Shannon in 1949
Practical limitations: 1) Key must be as long as message (storage issue), 2) Key distribution problem (same as symmetric), 3) Keys cannot be reused, 4) Truly random keys difficult to generate

questions.question: Describe the different modes of operation in block ciphers. Compare ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, and CTR modes with advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: ECB: Independent block encryption, parallelizable, but reveals patterns
CBC: Chained blocks with IV, sequential, better security, error propagation
CFB (Cipher Feedback): Turns block cipher into stream cipher, can encrypt partial blocks
OFB (Output Feedback): Similar to CFB but uses encrypted counter, less error propagation
CTR (Counter): Uses incrementing counter, parallelizable, no error propagation
Selection criteria depends on application requirements - CTR preferred for parallel processing

questions.question: Explain the evolution from DES to AES. Why was AES needed and how does it address the limitations of DES?
keyPoints: DES (1977) became insecure due to 56-bit key vulnerability (broken in 1998)
Triple DES (3DES) was interim solution but slower
AES selected through open competition (1997-2001), Rijndael algorithm chosen

AES advantages over DES: 1) Much larger key sizes (128/192/256 bits), 2) Larger block size (128 vs 64 bits), 3) Faster in software, 4) Designed to resist known attacks including differential and linear cryptanalysis, 5) More efficient in memory and computation
AES uses Substitution-Permutation Network vs DES Feistel structure
Rijndael won due to security, efficiency, flexibility, and simplicity
